blocks.content: Inspector Ray Davenport arrived at eleven thirty-eight.

He drove himself. A navy sedan, a little dented on the back quarter panel. He was a large man — not fat, just constructed on a bigger frame than most, the kind that made doorways notice him. Mid-fifties. A coat he'd had for too long. A hat he would have told you he wore for the cold, though it sat on his head with the ease of a habit that went back twenty years.
He stood outside the house for a moment before going in — just stood there, looking at the building the way he always did. New scene. Fresh eyes. Don't go in with a theory.
It was the first rule. The one he'd broken three times in thirty years and regretted all three.
The deputy — Briggs, young, still ironed — gave him the rundown in the hallway.

blocks.speaker: Deputy Briggs
blocks.content: Male, thirty-seven. Party of six, weekend stay. Found unresponsive at approximately ten-fifty by his housemates. Locked from the inside — they had to force the door. Paramedics confirm no pulse at eleven-oh-two. No visible trauma. Window was open. Medical history possibly includes blood pressure medication.

blocks.speaker: Deputy Briggs
blocks.content: Open all the way. Cold night, too. Forty-seven degrees.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: His housemates know it was open?

blocks.speaker: Deputy Briggs
blocks.content: One of them asked about it. The small woman, dark hair. She noticed when they first went in.
Davenport looked at the staircase. Then he went up.
